metadata.purpose: Architectural decision records (ADRs) for key technical choices made during Bend integration. Each record captures context, options considered, decision rationale, and consequences.

adr.title: Accumulative Diagnostics via Vec-based Collector
adr.context: Axon currently uses early-exit error handling (Result + ? operator) throughout the pipeline. Each compiler phase stops at the first error. Users see one error per compilation, requiring multiple compile cycles to fix all issues.

option.name: Vec-based Diagnostics collector
option.description: Simple Vec<Diagnostic> struct threaded through all phases. fatal() checkpoint returns Err only if errors accumulated.
option.pros: Simple. Matches Bend's approach. Easy to thread through existing phases. No concurrency concerns.
option.cons: Linear scan for fatal() check. Not thread-safe if compilation ever becomes parallel.

option.name: Channel-based diagnostic streaming
option.description: mpsc channel for diagnostics. Each phase sends diagnostics to a collector thread.
option.pros: Thread-safe by design. Decoupled producers and consumer.
option.cons: Over-engineered for single-threaded compiler. Channel overhead. Complex lifecycle.

option.name: Global mutable diagnostic store
option.description: Static mut or thread-local storage for diagnostics.
option.cons: Unsafe. Hard to test. Global state anti-pattern. Complicates parallel testing.

adr.rationale: Axon compilation is single-threaded. The Vec approach is simplest, matches Bend's proven pattern, and is trivially testable. If parallel compilation is ever needed, the struct can be wrapped in Mutex with minimal change.

consequences.positive: Simple implementation. Easy to test. Low overhead.
consequences.negative: Must thread &mut Diagnostics through all phase function signatures — moderate refactor.

adr.title: Fixed-Order PassManager with Trait Objects
adr.context: Axon needs a transformation pass pipeline for MIR optimizations (dead code, const fold, fusion, parallelism). Need to decide on pass registration, ordering, and extensibility approach.

option.name: Fixed-order Vec of trait objects
option.description: PassManager holds Vec<Box<dyn Pass>> in compile-time-determined order. Each pass is individually toggleable.
option.pros: Simple. Predictable. Easy to debug. Order is explicit and documented.
option.cons: Adding new passes requires modifying PassManager constructor. No dynamic reordering.

option.name: Dependency graph with topological sort
option.description: Passes declare dependencies. PassManager topologically sorts and executes.
option.pros: Flexible ordering. Passes can be added independently.
option.cons: Complex. Dependency cycles possible. Harder to debug ordering issues. Over-engineered for 7 passes.

option.name: Plugin system with dynamic loading
option.description: Passes as separate crates loaded via dlopen/dylib.
option.cons: Massive over-engineering. Platform-specific. ABI stability nightmare. We have 7 passes.

adr.decision: Option O1 — Fixed-order Vec of trait objects.
adr.rationale: 7 passes with well-defined order. Bend uses 31 passes in fixed order and it works. Simplicity is critical for correctness. The pass order (dead_code → const_fold → linearize → float_combinators → fuse_ops → inline → parallelize) has semantic dependencies that are clearer when explicit.

consequences.positive: Simple, predictable, debuggable. Each pass can be tested independently.
consequences.negative: New passes require code change in PassManager. Acceptable for a compiler with a small pass count.

adr.title: Pattern-Based Fusion with Hand-Written Kernels
adr.context: Need to fuse tensor operations to eliminate intermediate tensors. Options range from fully automatic fusion (like XLA) to pattern-based fusion with specific hand-written kernels.

option.name: Pattern-based with hand-written kernels
option.description: Define specific fusion patterns (linear+relu, mse, etc). Each pattern maps to a hand-written C kernel in runtime.
option.pros: Predictable. Numerically verifiable per-pattern. Easier to debug. Incremental — add patterns one at a time.
option.cons: Limited to known patterns. New patterns require new kernel code. Doesn't discover novel fusions.

option.name: Fully automatic tiling/fusion (XLA-style)
option.description: Analyze data flow, automatically determine fusion boundaries, generate fused kernels via code generation.
option.pros: Discovers novel fusions. Adapts to any operation combination.
option.cons: Enormously complex. Requires full cost model. Code generation is hard to verify numerically. XLA took years to build.

option.name: Polyhedral model (MLIR-style)
option.description: Represent tensor ops as polyhedral iteration spaces, apply affine transformations for fusion.
option.pros: Theoretically optimal. Handles complex loop nests.
option.cons: Requires MLIR integration. Massive infrastructure. Overkill for current tensor op set.

adr.decision: Option O1 — Pattern-based with hand-written kernels. O2/O3 deferred to EPSILON phase as design documents.
adr.rationale: 5 patterns cover the most impactful ML operation chains. 80/20 rule: these 5 patterns cover ~80% of the fusion benefit in typical ML workloads. Hand-written kernels are verifiable, debuggable, and numerically controllable. XLA-style fusion requires major infrastructure that doesn't exist yet.

consequences.positive: Achievable in BETA phase. Each pattern independently verifiable. Predictable performance.
consequences.negative: Won't discover novel fusions. Manual kernel writing for each new pattern. Acceptable trade-off for current maturity.

adr.title: CPU Thread Pool for Independent Tensor Operations
adr.context: Need automatic parallelism for independent tensor operations. Bend uses interaction net redex scheduling which is fundamentally different from Axon's imperative model.

option.name: CPU thread pool with DAG scheduling
option.description: Build DAG of tensor ops, identify independent subgraphs, dispatch to thread pool.
option.pros: Matches Axon's imperative model. Practical. Windows-compatible with #ifdef. Incremental benefit.
option.cons: Limited to data-level parallelism. Thread pool overhead for small ops.

option.name: Interaction net runtime (Bend-style)
option.description: Compile Axon to interaction combinators, use HVM2-style parallel reduction.
option.pros: Automatic parallelism for everything, not just tensor ops.
option.cons: Complete paradigm mismatch. Would require rewriting Axon's runtime. Impractical.

option.name: GPU stream scheduling
option.description: Map independent tensor ops to different CUDA/Metal streams.
option.pros: GPU-native parallelism. High throughput for large tensors.
option.cons: Requires GPU backend (not yet implemented). Not useful for CPU-only users.

adr.decision: Option O1 — CPU thread pool. Option O3 deferred to EPSILON.
adr.rationale: CPU thread pool is practical, incrementally beneficial, and works on Windows (primary dev platform). The DAG approach is a natural fit for tensor operations — each op has clear data dependencies. GPU stream scheduling requires the GPU backend, which is a separate project.

consequences.positive: Works today on Windows. No new runtime dependencies. Sequential fallback always available.
consequences.negative: CPU-only — GPU users won't see parallel benefit until EPSILON. Thread pool overhead may negate benefit for very small tensors (mitigated by size threshold).

adr.title: Opt-In Linear Annotations as Separate Analysis Pass
adr.context: Bend uses automatic affine variable semantics (variables used once are moved, multiple uses trigger auto-dup). Axon has an existing borrow checker. Need to decide how linearity integrates.

option.name: Opt-in linear keyword, separate pass
option.description: Add linear keyword for function parameters. Separate linearity checking pass runs after borrow checking. Non-annotated code unaffected.
option.pros: Backward compatible. Non-invasive. Clear opt-in semantics. Borrow checker unchanged.
option.cons: Users must manually annotate. Won't automatically optimize non-annotated code.

option.name: Automatic affine inference (Bend-style)
option.description: Automatically determine variable usage count. Single-use = move (zero-copy). Multiple use = auto-clone.
option.pros: No annotation burden. All code benefits automatically.
option.cons: Changes semantics of existing code. May introduce unexpected clones. Hard to integrate with borrow checker.

option.name: Integrate linearity into borrow checker
option.description: Extend borrow checker to track linearity as part of its core analysis.
option.pros: Unified analysis. Single pass for all ownership properties.
option.cons: High risk — modifying borrow checker could break existing behavior. Complex interaction with borrows, moves, copies.

adr.decision: Option O1 — Opt-in linear keyword, separate pass.
adr.rationale: RISK-06 identified high risk of linearity/borrow checker interaction. Separate pass minimizes risk. Opt-in is safe for pre-release language. Users who want zero-copy get it explicitly. Users who don't care are unaffected. Can evolve toward O2 later if desired.

consequences.positive: Zero risk to existing code. Clear documentation path ("add linear for zero-copy"). Testable independently.
consequences.negative: Optimization only when user opts in. Acceptable trade-off — ML-aware users will learn linear quickly.

adr.title: Platform-Agnostic Threading with #ifdef
adr.context: Primary development platform is Windows. C runtime needs a thread pool for auto-parallelism. pthreads is not native on Windows.

option.name: #ifdef for Windows CreateThread, else pthreads
option.description: Conditional compilation in C runtime: CreateThread on Windows, pthreads on Unix.
option.pros: Native on both platforms. No external dependencies. Well-understood pattern.

option.name: C11 threads (thrd_create)
option.description: Use C11 standard threading (thrd_create, mtx_lock, etc).
option.cons: MSVC has incomplete C11 thread support. May not work reliably on Windows.

option.name: Rust-side threading (rayon/crossbeam)
option.description: Move thread pool from C runtime to Rust side, use rayon or crossbeam.
option.cons: Adds Rust runtime dependency to compiled binary. Complicates C runtime ABI. FFI overhead for each dispatch.

adr.rationale: Runtime is already C. Platform #ifdef is standard practice in C codebases. Both CreateThread and pthreads are well-documented. Sequential fallback handles unsupported platforms. MSVC C11 thread support is unreliable, ruling out O2.

consequences.positive: Works on Windows. No new dependencies. Sequential fallback for safety.
consequences.negative: Two code paths to maintain. Acceptable for a thread pool (small API surface).

adr.title: Features Explicitly NOT Adopted from Bend
adr.context: Bend has many features, not all of which are appropriate for Axon. This ADR documents what was deliberately excluded and why.

feature.name: Interaction Combinator Runtime (HVM2)
feature.reason: Axon compiles to LLVM IR → native code. Replacing this with an interaction net runtime would require rewriting the entire backend. The performance characteristics are fundamentally different — IC is for massive parallelism of fine-grained operations, while Axon targets tensor-level parallelism.

feature.name: 24-bit Number Limitation
feature.reason: Bend uses u24/i24/f24 due to HVM2's 60-bit node format. Axon uses standard f32/f64/i32/i64. This is a fundamental limitation of Bend, not a feature.

feature.name: Dual Syntax (Imp/Fun)
feature.reason: Axon has one established syntax. Adding a second syntax multiplies maintenance and documentation burden without clear benefit. Bend's dual syntax exists because it targets both Python and Haskell audiences.

feature.name: Automatic Duplication (auto-dup)
feature.reason: Axon has explicit ownership and borrowing. Automatic duplication would conflict with the borrow checker and change semantics of existing programs. Opt-in linear annotations are safer.

feature.name: Scott/NumScott ADT Encoding
feature.reason: These are encoding strategies for interaction combinators. Axon's ADTs are already efficiently compiled to tagged unions via LLVM. Scott encoding would add overhead, not remove it.

feature.name: Bend/Fold Loop Replacements
feature.reason: Axon already has for/while loops. Bend's bend/fold replace loops entirely because interaction combinators have no mutable state. Axon has mutation, so standard loops are more appropriate. However, fold/reduce are adopted as additional functional primitives alongside existing loops.

feature.name: Optional HM Type System
feature.reason: Axon has a strong, non-optional type system. Making type-checking optional would undermine Axon's safety guarantees.

adr.rationale: Bend and Axon have fundamentally different execution models. Features that make sense for interaction combinators don't necessarily make sense for a compiled, imperative language with LLVM codegen. We adopt the ideas and patterns, not the specific implementations.

fields.field: Why is this decision needed? What's the problem?
